subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: A pendulum clock when taken to Mount Everest becomes fast.

Reason R: The value of g (acceleration due to gravity) is less at Mount Everest than its value on the surface of earth.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: A pendulum clock when taken to Mount Everest becomes fast.

Reason R: The value of g (acceleration due to gravity) is less at Mount Everest than its value on the surface of earth.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is not the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is not correct
solution:
Time period of a pendulum is given by T=2πLg, where L is the length of the pendulum and g is the acceleration due to gravity.

As, T∝1g, so the time period of vibration of the pendulum clock increases i.e. it starts taking more time to complete each vibration. Therefore, the pendulum clock becomes slow.

The acceleration due to gravity decreases with height h h≪R above the surface as:

g'=g1-hR 

Thus, value of g is less at Mount Everest than its value on the surface of earth.

Hence, assertion is wrong, but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
q: The mass and the diameter of a planet are three times the respective values for the Earth. The period of oscillation of a simple pendulum on the Earth is $2 \mathrm{~s}$. The period of oscillation of the same pendulum on the planet would be:
question: The mass and the diameter of a planet are three times the respective values for the Earth. The period of oscillation of a simple pendulum on the Earth is $2 \mathrm{~s}$. The period of oscillation of the same pendulum on the planet would be:
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity $\mathrm{g}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}$

$\frac{g_{p}}{g_{e}}=\frac{M_{p}}{M_{e}}\left(\frac{R_{e}}{R_{p}}\right)^{2}=3\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{3}$

Also $\mathrm{T} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{g}}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{p}}}}=\sqrt{3}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{p}}=2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~s}$

q: The radius in kilometer to which the present radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>)</mo></math> to be compressed so that the escape velocity is increased <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> time is _______.
question: The radius in kilometer to which the present radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>)</mo></math> to be compressed so that the escape velocity is increased <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> time is _______.
solution:
Ve=2GmR …1

10 Ve=2GmR' …2

∴10=RR'

⇒R'=R100=6400100=64 km

subject: Physics
q:
A satellite revolving around a planet in stationary orbit has time period 6 hours. The mass of planet is one-fourth the mass of earth. The radius orbit of planet is :
$\left(\right.$ Given $=$ Radius of geo-stationary orbit for earth is $4.2 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~km}$ )
question:
A satellite revolving around a planet in stationary orbit has time period 6 hours. The mass of planet is one-fourth the mass of earth. The radius orbit of planet is :
$\left(\right.$ Given $=$ Radius of geo-stationary orbit for earth is $4.2 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~km}$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{r}^{3 / 2}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{GM}}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}_1}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right)^{3 / 2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}_2}{\mathrm{M}_1}\right)^{1 / 2} \\ & \frac{6}{24}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{r}_1\right)^{3 / 2}}{\left(4.2 \times 10^4\right)^{3 / 2}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{M} / 4}\right)^{1 / 2} \\ & \mathrm{r}_1=1.05 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~km}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Two satellites A and B of masses 200 kg and 400 kg are revolving round the earth at height of 600 km and 1600 km respectively. If TA and TB are the time periods of A and B respectively then the value of TB-TA :

[ Given : radius of earth =6400 km, mass of earth =6×1024 kg ]
question:
Two satellites A and B of masses 200 kg and 400 kg are revolving round the earth at height of 600 km and 1600 km respectively. If TA and TB are the time periods of A and B respectively then the value of TB-TA :

[ Given : radius of earth =6400 km, mass of earth =6×1024 kg ]
solution:
⇒T=2πr3GM

⇒TA=2π6400+600×103GM

⇒TB=2π×10983GM

⇒TB-TA=2π109GM88-77

=314×4.107

=1289.64

=1.289×103 s

subject: Physics
q: A planet takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn></math> days to complete one revolution around the Sun. If the distance of the planet from Sun is reduced to one fourth of the original distance, how many days will it take to complete one revolution?
question: A planet takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn></math> days to complete one revolution around the Sun. If the distance of the planet from Sun is reduced to one fourth of the original distance, how many days will it take to complete one revolution?
solution:
The Law of Periods: The square of the period of any planet is proportional to the cube of the semimajor axis of its orbit.

T2∝r3

Therefore, we can write

T12r13=T22r23

⇒(200)2r3=T22r43

⇒200×2004×4×4=T22

⇒T2=25 days

subject: Physics
q: Two satellites <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> having masses in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math> are revolving in circular orbits of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>r</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>r</mi></math> respectively around the earth. The ratio of total mechanical energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is
question: Two satellites <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> having masses in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math> are revolving in circular orbits of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>r</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>r</mi></math> respectively around the earth. The ratio of total mechanical energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is
solution:
Given that m1 m2=43,r1r2=34

Now the total energy of a satellite TE=12mvo2+-GMmr

Since the orbital speed of a satellite vo=GMr

⇒TE=-GMm2r∝mr

⇒TE1TE2=m1 m2·r2r1=43×43=169

q: On the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> from the origin, the gravitational field due to a mass distribution is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>A</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mfrac></math> in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-direction. The magnitude of the gravitational potential on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>, taking its value to be zero at infinity is:
question: On the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> from the origin, the gravitational field due to a mass distribution is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>A</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mfrac></math> in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-direction. The magnitude of the gravitational potential on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>, taking its value to be zero at infinity is:
solution:
Vx=−∫∞xAxA2+x23/2−dx

Vx=-AA2+x2

subject: Physics
q: A satellite is in an elliptical orbit around a planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is observed that the velocity of the satellite when it is farthest from the planet is 6 times less than that when it is closest to the planet. The ratio of distances between the satellite and the planet at closest and farthest points is :
question: A satellite is in an elliptical orbit around a planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is observed that the velocity of the satellite when it is farthest from the planet is 6 times less than that when it is closest to the planet. The ratio of distances between the satellite and the planet at closest and farthest points is :
solution:
From angular momentum conservation

mV1r1=mV2r2

r1r2=v2v1=16

1:6

q: The angular momentum of a planet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> moving around the sun in an elliptical orbit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>L</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>. The magnitude of the areal velocity of the planet is :
question: The angular momentum of a planet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> moving around the sun in an elliptical orbit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>L</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>. The magnitude of the areal velocity of the planet is :
solution:
For small displacement ds of the planet its area can be written as

dA=12rdl

=12rdsinθ

A.vel =dAdt=12rsinθdsdt=Vrsinθ2

dAdt=12mVrsinθm=L2 m

q: Find the gravitational force of attraction between the ring and sphere as shown in the diagram, where the plane of the ring is perpendicular to the line joining the centres. If 8R is the distance between the centres of a ring (of mass m) and a sphere (mass M) where both have equal radius R
question: Find the gravitational force of attraction between the ring and sphere as shown in the diagram, where the plane of the ring is perpendicular to the line joining the centres. If 8R is the distance between the centres of a ring (of mass m) and a sphere (mass M) where both have equal radius R
solution:
Gravitational field of ring

=-GmxR2+x23/2

Force between sphere & ring

=GmM8RR2+8R23/2

=GmMR2×827

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: Earth has atmosphere whereas moon doesn’t have any atmosphere.

Reason R: The escape velocity on moon is very small as compared to that on earth.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: Earth has atmosphere whereas moon doesn’t have any atmosphere.

Reason R: The escape velocity on moon is very small as compared to that on earth.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>  is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>
solution:
The formula to calculate the escape velocity is given by

ve=2gR   ...(1)

As both the acceleration and radius of the Moon is much less than that of the Earth, the value of the escape velocity in Moon is much less than that on the Earth.

The average velocity of gas molecules remains higher than the escape velocity due to the lower escape velocity on the moon's surface, making the moon unable to maintain an atmosphere.

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is moving in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> about a planet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>. At some instant, it splits into two equal masses. The first mass moves in a circular orbit of radius <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> . And the other mass, in a circular orbit of radius <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The difference between the final and the initial total energies is
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is moving in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> about a planet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>. At some instant, it splits into two equal masses. The first mass moves in a circular orbit of radius <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> . And the other mass, in a circular orbit of radius <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The difference between the final and the initial total energies is
solution:
Ei=-GMm2R,

Ef=-GMm/22R2-GMm/223R2=-GMm2R-GMm6R=-4GMm6R=-2GMm3R

Ef-Ei= GMm2R-23+12= -GMm6R.

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass is taken from earth surface to the height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> equal to twice the radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>)</mo></math>, the increase in potential energy will be : (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo></math> acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth)
question: A body of mass is taken from earth surface to the height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> equal to twice the radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>)</mo></math>, the increase in potential energy will be : (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo></math> acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth)
solution:
The potential energy of a particle at a distance r (>Re)  from the centre of Earth is,

U=-GMemr

Initially, the particle is at a height Re from the surface. Therefore, r=Re.

∴Ui=-GMemRe

When height is increased to 2Re from surface, r=2Re+Re=3Re

∴Uf=-GMem3Re

Thus, the change in potential energy is

∆U=Uf-Ui=-GMem3Re+GMemRe=23GMemRe=23GMeRe2mRe=23mgRe as g=GMeRe2

subject: Physics
q: The height of any point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> above the surface of earth is equal to diameter of earth. The value of acceleration due to gravity at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> will be : (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo></math>acceleration due to gravity at the surface of earth).
question: The height of any point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> above the surface of earth is equal to diameter of earth. The value of acceleration due to gravity at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> will be : (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo></math>acceleration due to gravity at the surface of earth).
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at a height h from the surface of the Earth,
g'=g11+hR2
Given: h=2R
∴g'=g11+22
or g'=g9

subject: Physics
q: The minimum and maximum distances of a planet revolving around the Sun are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If the minimum speed of the planet on its trajectory is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>, then its maximum speed will be:
question: The minimum and maximum distances of a planet revolving around the Sun are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If the minimum speed of the planet on its trajectory is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>, then its maximum speed will be:
solution:
Angular momentum conservation equation, 

v0x2=v1x1

v1=v0x2x1

subject: Physics
q: Earth has mass 8 times and radius 2 times that of a planet. If the escape velocity from the earth is $11.2 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$, the escape velocity in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{s}$ from the planet will be :
question: Earth has mass 8 times and radius 2 times that of a planet. If the escape velocity from the earth is $11.2 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$, the escape velocity in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{s}$ from the planet will be :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}_{\text {escape }}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}}} \\ & \frac{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\text {escape }}\right)_{\text {Planet }}}{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\text {escape }}\right)_{\text {Earth }}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{E}}}\right) \times\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{P}}}\right)}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \left(\mathrm{~V}_{\text {escape }}\right)_{\text {Planet }}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{~V}_{\text {escape }}\right)_{\text {Earth }}=5.6 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Suppose the gravitational force varies inversely as the nth power of distance. Then the time period planet in circular orbit of radius $\mathrm{R}$ around the sun will be proportional to
question: Suppose the gravitational force varies inversely as the nth power of distance. Then the time period planet in circular orbit of radius $\mathrm{R}$ around the sun will be proportional to
solution: $\mathrm{T} \propto \mathrm{R}^{(\mathrm{n}+1) / 2}$

subject: Physics
q: A space ship of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is launched into a circular orbit close to the earth surface. The additional velocity to be imparted to the space ship in the orbit to overcome the gravitational pull will be (if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> ):
question: A space ship of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is launched into a circular orbit close to the earth surface. The additional velocity to be imparted to the space ship in the orbit to overcome the gravitational pull will be (if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and radius of earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> ):
solution:
The orbital velocity of satellite is 

vorb=GMR

The escape velocity is 

vesc=2GMR

Hence, the additional velocity required is 

Δv=vesc-vorb=2GMR-GMR=(2-1)GMR
=(2-1)gR
=(2-1)10×6400×103
=8(2-1) km s-1

subject: Physics
q: What is the minimum energy required to launch a satellite of mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math> from the surface of a planet of mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>M</mtext> </mrow> </math> and radius <math> <mrow> <mtext>R</mtext> </mrow> </math> in a circular orbit at an altitude of <math> <mrow> <mtext>2R</mtext> </mrow> </math>?
question: What is the minimum energy required to launch a satellite of mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math> from the surface of a planet of mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>M</mtext> </mrow> </math> and radius <math> <mrow> <mtext>R</mtext> </mrow> </math> in a circular orbit at an altitude of <math> <mrow> <mtext>2R</mtext> </mrow> </math>?
solution:
As we know,
Gravitational potential cnergy =-GMmr
and orbital velocity, v0=GM/R+h
Ef=12mv02-GMm3R=12 mGM3R-GMm3R
=GMm3R12-1=-GMm6R
Ei=-GMmR+K
Ei=Ef
Therefore minimum required energy,
K=5GMm6R

subject: Physics
q: If earth has a mass nine times and radius twice to the of a planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>3</mn></mfrac><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be the minimum velocity required by a rocket to pull out of gravitational force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, where ve is escape velocity on earth. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: If earth has a mass nine times and radius twice to the of a planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>3</mn></mfrac><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be the minimum velocity required by a rocket to pull out of gravitational force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, where ve is escape velocity on earth. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
The formula to calculate the escape velocityve for the Earth is given by

ve= 2GMeRe.......................(1)

where, G is the Universal Gravitational constant, Me is the mass and Re is the radius of Earth.

The formula for the escape velocity vesc of any planet is given by

vesc= 2GMR........................(2)

where, M is the mass and R is the radius of the planet.

Given that the mass of the planet is 9 times the mass of the Earth and the radius of the planet is 2 times the radius of the Earth.

Substitute Me9 for M and Re2 for R into equation (2) and simplify to obtain the escape velocity for the planet in terms of that in Earth.

vesc= 2GMe9Re2= 292GMeRe= 23ve.....................(3)

Comparing equation (3) with the given expression, it can be written that

x= 2.

subject: Physics
q:
The ratio of escape velocity of a planet to the escape velocity of earth will be:-

Given: Mass of the planet is 16 times mass of earth and radius of the planet is 4 times the radius of earth.
question:
The ratio of escape velocity of a planet to the escape velocity of earth will be:-

Given: Mass of the planet is 16 times mass of earth and radius of the planet is 4 times the radius of earth.
solution:
The formula to calculate the escape velocity is given by

ve=2GMR   ...1

For the Earth, it is given by

veE=2GMERE...2

 and, for the planet, it is given by

veP=2GMPRP...3

Divide equation (2) by equation (3) and simplify to obtain the required ratio.

veEveP=MEMP×RPRE   ...4

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (4) to obtain the required ratio.

veEveP=ME16ME×4RERE=12⇒veP:veE= 2:1

subject: Physics
q: A satellite is launched into a circular orbit of radius ' R ' around the earth. A second satellite is launched into an orbit of radius 1.03 R . The time period of revolution of the second satellite is larger than the first one approximately by
question: A satellite is launched into a circular orbit of radius ' R ' around the earth. A second satellite is launched into an orbit of radius 1.03 R . The time period of revolution of the second satellite is larger than the first one approximately by
solution: $T_1 \propto(R)^{3 / 2}$ and $T_2 \propto(1.03 R)^{3 / 2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad T_2=(1.03 R)^{3 / 2} \cdot T_1 \approx 1.045 T_1$ So $T_2$ will larger by $4.5 \%$ w.r.t. $T_1$.

subject: Physics
q: An object is taken to a height above the surface of earth at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> from the centre of the earth. Where radius of earth, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The percentage decrease in the weight of the object will be
question: An object is taken to a height above the surface of earth at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> from the centre of the earth. Where radius of earth, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The percentage decrease in the weight of the object will be
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at height h is given by, g'=g1+hR2

For distance from the centre r=54R height h=R4,

⇒g'=g1+142=1625g

Change in weight 

∆W=mg-mg'⇒∆W=mg1-1625

⇒∆W=925mg

Percentage change =925×100%

=36%

subject: Physics
q: A geostationary satellite is orbiting around an arbitrary planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> at a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mi>R</mi></math> above the surface of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> being the radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>. The time period of another satellite in hours at a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math> from the surface of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is ________ has the time period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi>hours</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A geostationary satellite is orbiting around an arbitrary planet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> at a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mi>R</mi></math> above the surface of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> being the radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>. The time period of another satellite in hours at a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math> from the surface of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is ________ has the time period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi>hours</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
T∝R3/2

24 T=12R3R3/2⇒T=3 hr

q: Which of the following most closely depicts the correct variation of the gravitation potential, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></math> with distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> due to a large planet of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and uniform mass density? (figures are not drawn to scale)
question: Which of the following most closely depicts the correct variation of the gravitation potential, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></math> with distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> due to a large planet of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and uniform mass density? (figures are not drawn to scale)
solution: At inside points <math><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo><</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, <math><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>G</mi><mi>M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><msup><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>. So at the centre,   <math><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>⇒</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mfrac><mrow><mi>G</mi><mi>M</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfrac><mi> </mi></math>. At outside points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi><mo>></mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>G</mi><mi>M</mi></mrow><mi>r</mi></mfrac></math>. At surface, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>G</mi><mi>M</mi></mrow><mi>R</mi></mfrac></math>. So the gravitational potential is non-uniform and negative.

subject: Physics
q: If a satellite orbiting the Earth is 9 times closer to the Earth than the Moon, what is the time period of rotation of the satellite? Given rotational time period of Moon $=27$ days and gravitational attraction between the satellite and the moon is neglected.
question: If a satellite orbiting the Earth is 9 times closer to the Earth than the Moon, what is the time period of rotation of the satellite? Given rotational time period of Moon $=27$ days and gravitational attraction between the satellite and the moon is neglected.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}^2 \propto \mathrm{R}^3 \\ & \left(\frac{\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{s}}}\right)^2=\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R} / 9}\right)^3 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{s}}}=(3)^3 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{s}}=\left(\frac{27}{27}\right)=1 \text { day }\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two planets A and B of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>R</mi></math> have densities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>ρ</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> respectively. The ratio of acceleration due to gravity at the surface of B to A is:
question: Two planets A and B of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>R</mi></math> have densities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>ρ</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> respectively. The ratio of acceleration due to gravity at the surface of B to A is:
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity is 

g=GMR2=4πGRρ3⇒g∝ρ·R

Thus, the ratio is 

⇒gBgA=1.5Rρ2ρR=12×1.5=0.75⇒gBgA=34
